title: Food & Drink
subtitle: How to eat well in Ireland — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Espresso culture is newer here
items.text: Ireland has embraced quality coffee relatively recently, so expect excellent espresso in Dublin, Cork, and Galway, but instant coffee still appears in smaller towns and rural pubs. If you're particular about your coffee, stick to independent cafés rather than chains.

items.label: Tea is still king
items.text: Irlandans drink far more tea than coffee, and they take it seriously—usually Barry's or Lyon's brand, milky and hot. If offered tea, accept it; it's a genuine gesture of hospitality.

items.label: 3fe Coffee (Dublin)
items.text: A third-wave coffee roastery in Dublin's Smithfield with single-origin beans and skilled baristas. Genuinely excellent and worth the queue on weekends.

items.label: Badger & Dodo (Cork)
items.text: A cosy independent café serving specialty coffee and homemade pastries in a converted Victorian building. Popular with locals and a good escape from tourist areas.

items.label: Colcannon
items.text: Mashed potatoes mixed with shredded cabbage or kale, butter, and scallions. It's humble comfort food, best served with a fried egg on top and a knob of butter melting into it.

items.label: Boxty
items.text: A potato pancake from Northern Ireland and the border counties, made with grated raw potato and flour, fried until crispy. Order it filled with meat stew or smoked salmon for a proper meal.

items.label: Seafood chowder
items.text: A creamy soup loaded with local fish, mussels, and prawns. Kinsale in Cork is famous for it, and it's best eaten in a waterfront pub with brown bread.

items.label: Bacon & cabbage
items.text: Often mistaken for corned beef and cabbage (an Irish-American invention), the Irish version uses boiled bacon rashers with white cabbage and parsley sauce. It's the real St. Brigid's Day dish.

items.label: Soda bread
items.text: Dense, slightly sweet bread made with baking soda instead of yeast, best eaten warm with butter. The brown soda bread is denser and more common than the white version.

items.label: Black pudding
items.text: A breakfast staple made from pork blood, offal, and oatmeal. Don't be squeamish—it's rich, slightly sweet, and delicious fried alongside eggs and bacon at a full Irish breakfast.

items.label: Pub culture (the real thing)
items.text: Pubs are the heart of Irish dining and socializing. Look for neighborhood pubs with locals playing cards or dominoes—these serve honest, affordable food and feel authentic. Avoid Temple Bar in Dublin and similar tourist pubs unless you want loud crowds and premium prices.

items.label: Michelin-starred restaurants in Dublin
items.text: Dublin has serious fine dining: Eleven Forty One, Éclat, and Sole all hold Michelin stars. Book ahead and expect creative modern Irish cuisine at London-equivalent prices.

items.label: Lunch is better value than dinner
items.text: Many restaurants offer excellent set lunch menus at half the dinner price. Eating your main meal at lunch and having lighter fare in the evening is both budget-friendly and culturally normal.

items.label: Food markets and delis
items.text: English Market in Cork and Temple Bar Food Market in Dublin sell fresh produce, cheese, smoked fish, and ready-made meals. Grab lunch here to eat like a local and avoid restaurant markup.

items.label: Avoid tourist-trap restaurants
items.text: Stay away from places with picture menus, those advertising 'authentic Irish food' in multiple languages, and anything within two blocks of major monuments. The best meals are in neighborhood spots where Irish people actually eat.

items.label: Rashers, rashers, rashers
items.text: A 'rasher' is a slice of bacon (closer to back bacon than American crispy bacon). 'Bacon & eggs' on an Irish menu means rashers, not American-style strips.

items.label: Chips are fries
items.text: When you see 'chips' on an Irish menu, it means french fries. 'Crisps' are what Americans call potato chips. A 'spice burger' is a flattened burger served on a bun—order it in a chipper (fish & chips shop), not a restaurant.

items.label: Coddle, stew, and hotpot
items.text: 'Coddle' (Dublin) is layered potatoes and sausages, 'Irish stew' is mutton and potatoes, and 'hotpot' varies by region. These are all hearty, one-dish meals perfect for cold weather.

items.label: Brown bread comes with everything
items.text: Restaurants automatically serve brown soda bread with soups and chowders. It's included, not a separate charge. Butter it generously.

items.label: Full Irish breakfast terms
items.text: 'Brekkie' or 'fry' means the cooked breakfast with rashers, sausages, black pudding, eggs, tomato, and mushrooms. 'Continental' means toast and pastries. Always ask for 'brown bread' if you want it instead of white.

items.label: Guinness is the default
items.text: Guinness is ubiquitous and genuinely better in Dublin (especially at the Guinness Storehouse or any pub near the brewery). However, Irish craft beer has exploded—try Galway Hooker, Smithwicks, or local breweries like Eight Degrees in Cork.

items.label: A pint takes time to pour
items.text: Guinness and other stouts are poured slowly in two stages to settle. Don't rush the bartender or ask them to speed up. This is ritual and part of the experience.

items.label: Irish whiskey renaissance
items.text: Irish whiskey production has exploded in the past decade. Jameson is the most famous, but try Redbreast, Green Spot, or Bushmills for something more interesting. Whiskey is drunk neat or with a splash of water, rarely with ice.

items.label: Wine is imported and pricey
items.text: Ireland doesn't produce wine, so imported bottles are expensive. Order wine by the glass rather than the bottle unless you're splurging. Beer or whiskey offer better value.

items.label: Irish coffee in its birthplace
items.text: Foynes Flying Boat Museum café and Dromoland Castle both claim to serve the original Irish coffee (whiskey, coffee, brown sugar, cream). It's best drunk slowly after dinner, not as an afternoon pick-me-up.

items.label: Closing times are real
items.text: Pubs close at 11:30 PM on weeknights and 12:30 AM on weekends. There's no 'last call'—the bar just stops serving. Plan accordingly.

items.label: Reservations are essential in cities
items.text: Dublin, Cork, and Galway restaurants, especially at dinner, require advance booking. Walk-ins may wait 90 minutes or be turned away. Rural pubs rarely need reservations.

items.label: Tipping is 10–15%, not mandatory
items.text: Tipping is appreciated but not required in Ireland. For good service, leave 10–15% or round up. Tip less in casual pubs, more in restaurants. Card machines often ask if you want to add a tip—you can decline.

items.label: Meal timing is different
items.text: 'Dinner' means the evening meal (7–9 PM), 'lunch' is midday, and 'breakfast' is morning. If someone invites you for 'tea,' they usually mean an early evening meal around 5–6 PM, not hot tea with pastries.

items.label: Pub etiquette: order at the bar
items.text: In casual pubs, order and pay at the bar—there's rarely table service unless it's a restaurant-pub hybrid. Sitting down doesn't mean someone will come take your order. Exception: upscale restaurants have table service.

items.label: Conversation is currency
items.text: In pubs, brief chats with strangers are normal and welcome. A simple 'How's the day treating you?' can lead to recommendations and local knowledge. Don't be shy.

items.label: Sunday roast is sacred
items.text: Many Irish families eat a roast dinner (usually beef, chicken, or lamb with potatoes and veg) on Sunday afternoons. Restaurants are busier on Sundays for this reason. Booking is essential.

items.label: Supermarkets for budget eating
items.text: Dunnes Stores, Tesco, and Lidl are everywhere and cheap. Buy lunch items, snacks, and breakfast here rather than at cafés. Their deli counters also make fresh sandwiches for under €6.

items.label: Dietary restrictions are understood
items.text: Vegetarian and vegan options are widely available in cities and tourist areas. Coeliac (gluten-free) restaurants exist in Dublin and Cork. Tell your server upfront; Irish restaurants are accommodating.

items.label: Breakfast is served late
items.text: Most cafés don't open until 8–9 AM, and breakfast is served until 11 AM or later. There's no American-style early breakfast culture. Plan accordingly if you're an early riser.
